lecture_title: Impact Analysis, Software Processes & Continuous Integration

questions.stem: What is the primary purpose of the Impact Analysis phase?

options.text: Identifying the potential consequences of a change and estimating what needs to be modified to accomplish it
Locating the first class where the change must begin
Writing the new behaviour required by the change request
Confirming that all tests pass after the change is complete

questions.explanation: IA determines the strategy and impact of change: 'identifying the potential consequences of a change, or estimating what needs to be modified to accomplish a change.' B is Concept Location, C is Actualization, D is Verification. (AnalysisLab p.1; ImpactAnalysis p.3)

questions.stem: Why is the result of Impact Analysis called the 'estimated' impact set?

options.text: It is a prediction of what will change, made before the code is edited, and may be wrong
It is calculated by a statistical estimator over historical commits
It estimates the monetary cost of the change in developer-hours
It is an approximation that is always larger than the true impact set

questions.explanation: The word 'estimated' signals it is a prediction made before editing; it can be too large or too small, and Verification later catches gaps. B/C invent mechanisms not in the deck; D is wrong because the estimate can be too small as well as too large. (ImpactAnalysis p.3-4)

questions.stem: The deck gives two flavors of interaction between classes. What are they?
questions.explanation: Interactions come as a dependency (a contract: one depends on the other) or coordination (they share coding/schedule/dataflow). B/C/D are real OO terms but are not the deck's two interaction flavors. (ImpactAnalysis p.5)

questions.stem: Why is bidirectionality the defining property of an interaction for Impact Analysis?

options.text: Change can propagate from A to B or from B to A, so IA cannot just follow dependency arrows one way
Because every dependency is automatically mutual in Java
Because the interaction graph stores each edge twice for performance
Because undirected graphs are easier to draw than directed ones

questions.explanation: Interactions propagate change in both directions, which is why IA walks the interaction graph rather than following dependency arrows one way. B is false (dependencies are directional); C/D are irrelevant rationalizations. (ImpactAnalysis p.5)

questions.stem: Why would marking a true mailman class as UNCHANGED be a mistake?

options.text: It would wrongly stop propagation, leaving its other neighbors uninspected even though change passes through it
It would cause the loop to never terminate
UNCHANGED classes still expand the frontier, so nothing would change
It would add the class to the estimated impact set incorrectly

questions.explanation: A mailman needs no code change yet still forces neighbors to be inspected; marking it UNCHANGED stops that propagation prematurely. The loop still terminates (B wrong); UNCHANGED does NOT expand the frontier (C wrong); UNCHANGED is excluded from the impact set, so D is the opposite error. (ImpactAnalysis p.10, p.12)

questions.stem: Besides finding impacted classes, what additional decision does Impact Analysis support?

options.text: Weighing alternative places to make the change
Choosing which programmer is assigned the change
Writing the unit tests for the change

questions.explanation: IA also weighs alternative locations for the change, surfacing the trade-offs. Assignment (B) and baselines (C) are process concerns; test-writing (D) belongs to later phases. (ImpactAnalysis p.14)

questions.stem: In the Fahrenheit-to-Celsius decision, which option favors long-term clarity at the cost of higher immediate effort?

options.text: Converting temperature centrally so all calculations live in one place
Changing only the user interface display
Adding a second display widget for Celsius
Leaving the code unchanged and documenting the difference

questions.explanation: Central conversion costs more now but keeps temperature logic in one place, improving long-term clarity and cheapening future changes. The UI-only patch (B) is the low-effort/low-clarity option; C/D are not the deck's alternatives. (ImpactAnalysis p.15)

questions.stem: In the SB5-MAI labs, the Analysis Lab applies the IA marking algorithm to which target codebase?

options.text: A real Java codebase (in this course, the JHotDraw-based project)
The cash-register store program from the slides

questions.explanation: The cash-register names are pedagogical stand-ins on the slides; the lab runs the same mechanics over the real JHotDraw-based Java codebase. The store program is only illustrative (B); the lab uses real code not pseudocode (C); Bugzilla appears only as an interruption example (D). (AnalysisLab p.1)

questions.stem: What running example domain do the Lecture-3 slides actually use to illustrate interactions and processes?

options.text: A cash-register / store domain (Store, Inventory, Item, Price, Cashiers)

questions.explanation: None of the Lecture-3 decks name JHotDraw on a slide; the running example is the cash-register/store domain. JHotDraw is the lab's applied target, not the slide example. (ImpactAnalysis p.7-8)

questions.stem: In the Analysis Lab portfolio table, what does each student record after running concept location and IA?

options.text: Packages visited and the number of classes visited
The exact LOC of every changed file
The defect density of the codebase

questions.explanation: The lab's Table 1 tabulates package name, number of classes, and comments for the classes visited. LOC (B), defect density (C), and the Manifesto (D) are not what this table records. (AnalysisLab p.2)

questions.stem: On the granularity hierarchy, at what level is 'software change' classified?
questions.explanation: A software change is a task (alongside acceptance testing). Processes are SIP/AIP/DIP; subtasks/phases are concept location/actualization; steps/actions are class inspections. (IntroSwProcesses p.4)

questions.stem: On the granularity hierarchy, at what level are 'concept location' and 'actualization' classified?
questions.explanation: Concept location and actualization are subtasks/phases of the software-change task. The change itself is a task; SIP/AIP/DIP are processes; class inspection is a step. (IntroSwProcesses p.4)

questions.stem: An exam answer labels 'acceptance testing' as a process. Why is that a granularity error?

options.text: Acceptance testing is a task, not a process; processes are SIP/AIP/DIP
Acceptance testing is a lifecycle, not a process
Acceptance testing is a step, not a process

questions.explanation: Acceptance testing is listed at the task level alongside software change; processes are SIP/AIP/DIP. It is neither a lifecycle (B) nor a single step (C), so D is wrong too. (IntroSwProcesses p.4)

questions.stem: In the SIP enactment table, which phase produces the comment 'Estimated set has 4 classes'?
questions.explanation: The estimated set with 4 classes is the output of IA. CL located CashierRecord; Act replaces the class; Base produces the baseline. (IntroSwProcesses p.11)

questions.stem: In the SIP enactment table, the 'CL' step lands on which class?
questions.explanation: Concept location lands on CashierRecord. Session is the class extracted in Ref and replaced in Act; Item and Price belong to the IA example, not this CL step. (IntroSwProcesses p.11)

questions.stem: In the SIP enactment table, the step 'Ref - Extract class Session' demonstrates that refactoring happens where relative to actualization?

options.text: Before actualization (prefactoring), preparing the code to receive the change

questions.explanation: Ref (extract class Session) precedes Act (replace class Session), i.e. prefactoring prepares a clean landing site before the actual change. B/C/D contradict the table's ordering. (IntroSwProcesses p.11)

questions.stem: How many significant digits of a LOC measure are meaningful, per the deck?

options.text: Only the one or two most significant digits (e.g. 900 LOC, 23 KLOC, 3.2 MLOC)

questions.explanation: Only the one or two most significant digits are meaningful, so you write 23 KLOC, not 23,418 LOC. B/C overstate precision; D understates LOC's usefulness. (IntroSwProcesses p.16-17)

questions.stem: In AIP, how are decisions made?
questions.explanation: AIP decisions are made by consensus. Management directive characterizes DIP; the architect and build server do not decide in AIP. (TeamProcesses p.4)

questions.stem: In CIP, before developers commit, whose permission must they obtain?
questions.explanation: CIP requires permission to commit from architects and code owners. The product manager handles strategy; users don't gate commits; D describes an unsafeguarded process. (TeamProcesses p.16)

questions.stem: How does CI's stance on code ownership contrast with the team-processes notion of code ownership?

options.text: CI says the team owns the code, not the individual, whereas code ownership has programmers specialize in parts of the code
CI requires individual ownership of every file
Code ownership means no one owns the code

questions.explanation: CI holds that the team owns the code (not the individual), contrasting with code ownership where programmers specialize in parts of the code. B/C/D misstate the contrast. (ContinuousIntegration p.5; TeamProcesses p.17)

questions.stem: How does the deck define Continuous Integration?

options.text: Teams integrate their work multiple times per day, each integration verified by an automated build
Teams integrate once at the end of the project
Each developer keeps a private branch indefinitely
Integration is done manually by a release manager monthly

questions.explanation: CI means integrating many times per day with each integration verified by an automated build. B/C/D describe the opposite of CI. (ContinuousIntegration p.2)

questions.stem: Which does the CI deck name as the MOST effective defect-discovery methods?

options.text: Design inspections, code inspections, and testing

questions.explanation: The most effective methods are design inspections, code inspections, and testing. B/C/D are not the deck's named most-effective methods. (ContinuousIntegration p.8)

questions.stem: Why does the CI deck recommend multiple quality methods rather than relying on one?

options.text: Multiple quality methods discover more defects than any single method
Multiple methods are cheaper than one

questions.explanation: Multiple methods discover more defects, since individuals are under 50% efficient alone. B contradicts the data; C/D are not stated. (ContinuousIntegration p.8)

questions.stem: According to the deck, why are unit tests the best method for verifying builds?

options.text: They are fast (no database or file system), focused (pinpoint problems)
They are slow but thorough end-to-end checks
They require a full production environment

questions.explanation: Unit tests are fast (no I/O) and focused, making them best for verifying builds. End-to-end/slow describes system tests (B); C/D are false. (ContinuousIntegration p.9)

questions.stem: How do system tests differ from unit tests in the CI deck's self-testing-build discussion?

options.text: System tests are end-to-end and often take minutes to hours; unit tests are fast and focused
System tests are faster than unit tests
Unit tests are end-to-end; system tests are focused

questions.explanation: System tests are end-to-end and slow (minutes to hours); unit tests are fast and focused. B/C reverse the properties; D is false. (ContinuousIntegration p.9)

questions.stem: What is a 'self-testing build' in CI?

options.text: A build that runs its own tests (especially unit tests) and is the best method for verifying builds
A build that skips all tests for speed
A build that only compiles, never tests
A build run only on the developer's laptop

questions.explanation: A self-testing build runs its own tests (especially unit tests) and is the best way to verify a build. B/C remove testing; D misplaces it. (ContinuousIntegration p.9)

questions.stem: Which of these is listed as a test framework in the CI deck?
questions.explanation: JUnit is a test framework (with NUnit, MSTest, Selenium, FitNesse). Cobertura is coverage; Checkstyle and Findbugs are static analysis. (ContinuousIntegration p.12)

questions.stem: Which set are all named as test frameworks in the deck?
questions.explanation: A lists the test frameworks. B lists static-analysis tools; C lists coverage tools; D mixes build tools and a tracker. (ContinuousIntegration p.12)

questions.stem: Combining the CI deck's figures, why is a self-testing build with multiple quality methods recommended over trusting the author's own checking?

options.text: Individuals are under 50% efficient at finding their own bugs, but 3+ methods reach over 90% defect removal
Authors find 100% of their own bugs, so methods are redundant
A single method already removes over 90% of defects
Self-testing builds remove the need for any inspection

questions.explanation: Because individuals are under 50% efficient alone, combining 3 or more methods (inspections + testing) reaches over 90% defect removal. B/C contradict the figures; D ignores that inspections remain a top method. (ContinuousIntegration p.8)

questions.stem: Where does Impact Analysis sit in the eight-phase change process?

options.text: Third, after Concept Location and before Prefactoring

questions.explanation: IA is the third phase: Initiation, Concept Location, Impact Analysis, Prefactoring, and so on. B/C/D misplace it. (ImpactAnalysis p.3)

questions.stem: In the SIP enactment table, which abbreviation denotes the impact-analysis task?
questions.explanation: IA denotes impact analysis. CL is concept location, Act is actualization, Base is baseline. (IntroSwProcesses p.11)

questions.stem: You join a large project where managers direct work, programmers hold specialised roles (tester, architect), and additionally nobody may commit without an architect's or code owner's permission. Which process is this?
questions.explanation: The explicit 'permission to commit' gate via architects/code owners is CIP's signature feature on top of DIP-style direction and specialisation. (TeamProcesses p.15-16)

questions.stem: You are the programmer in the Interactive IA swimlane. The computer hands you a NEXT class. Which decision is yours to make rather than the computer's?

options.text: Whether the class is INSPECTED, PROPAGATING, or CHANGED
Maintaining the queue of NEXT classes

questions.explanation: In Interactive IA the computer manages state (BLANK init, the NEXT queue, applying marks) while the programmer supplies the judgement of each selected class. (ImpactAnalysis p.16)

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT one of the five principles of Continuous Integration?

options.text: Deploy automatically to production after every commit

questions.explanation: The five principles are stability-based environments, maintain a repository, commit frequently/build every commit, self-testing build, keep the build fast. Auto-deploy to prod is not among them. (ContinuousIntegration p.3)

questions.stem: A developer keeps saying 'but it builds on my box.' Which CI arrangement is designed precisely to settle this dispute?

options.text: The build server, which only takes code from the repo and is the final authority
Letting each developer self-certify their machine
Running builds only weekly to reduce conflicts

questions.explanation: CI makes the repo the source of record and the build server the final authority, defeating the 'works on my machine' excuse. (ContinuousIntegration p.6)

questions.stem: A student claims 'IA should always pick the lowest-effort change location.' Why is this mistaken?

options.text: The two criteria (effort and clarity) genuinely conflict; least effort often scatters logic and hurts long-term clarity
Lowest effort is always best for clarity too

questions.explanation: Effort and clarity conflict; the lowest-effort patch (e.g. UI-only) often harms clarity, so IA surfaces both options with costs. (ImpactAnalysis p.15)

questions.stem: In the SIP enactment table, which ordering of phase abbreviations is correct for one change?

options.text: Ini, Pri, IA, CL, Ref, Act, Ex, Base
Pri, Ini, CL, IA, Ref, Ex, Act, Base
CL, Pri, IA, Ini, Act, Ref, Base, Ex
IA, CL, Ini, Pri, Base, Act, Ref, Ex

questions.explanation: The enactment sequence is Pri (prioritise), Ini, CL, IA, Ref, Ex, Act, Base. (IntroSwProcesses p.11)

questions.stem: In the SB5-MAI labs, against which target codebase do students run the IA marking algorithm?
questions.explanation: The Analysis Lab runs the Fig 7.9 IA algorithm against the JHotDraw Java codebase; the store names on slides are pedagogical stand-ins. (AnalysisLab p.1)

questions.stem: In the CI Lab, what infrastructure do students stand up for their (JHotDraw-based) project?

options.text: GitHub Actions workflow (.yml) plus a Maven build with tests

questions.explanation: The CI Lab has students add a GitHub Actions .yml and Maven build/tests, plus .maven-settings.xml for shared jars. (CILab p.1)

questions.stem: You want to commit small, functional changes with unit tests and remember that the team, not you, owns the code. Which CI principle are you practising?

options.text: Commit frequently and build every commit

questions.explanation: Committing small functional changes with unit tests, team-owned code, is the 'commit frequently / build every commit' principle. (ContinuousIntegration p.5)

questions.stem: Which is NOT an input or output of Impact Analysis?

options.text: Class dependencies analysed to add impacted classes

questions.explanation: IA consumes the initial set and produces the estimated set by analysing dependencies; it does not take or produce a compiled binary. (ImpactAnalysis p.3)

questions.stem: The SIP enactment step 'Ref - Extract class Session' demonstrates that refactoring of this kind occurs at what point relative to actualization?
questions.explanation: 'Ref - Extract class Session' precedes 'Act - Replace class Session', i.e. prefactoring before actualization. (IntroSwProcesses p.11)

questions.stem: The SIP enactment table walks one change through eight abbreviations. What does this demonstrate about the change process?

options.text: A software change is a repeatable task composed of ordered phases including IA

questions.explanation: The Pri/Ini/CL/IA/Ref/Ex/Act/Base walk shows the change as an ordered, repeatable task with IA as one phase. (IntroSwProcesses p.11)

questions.stem: In the Analysis Lab, after concept location students tabulate what about the codebase they explored?

options.text: Packages and the number of classes visited

questions.explanation: The portfolio table records package names and the number of classes visited (with comments). (AnalysisLab p.1-2)

questions.stem: Why does the guide treat the cash-register class names on the slides as stand-ins when applying IA to JHotDraw?

options.text: The store domain replaces JHotDraw in the exam
JHotDraw is not a Java codebase
The slides use the store domain pedagogically; the lab runs the same algorithm on the real JHotDraw graph
JHotDraw literally contains Store and Cashier classes

questions.explanation: Store/Cashier names are pedagogical; the Analysis Lab transfers the same marking mechanics to JHotDraw's interaction graph. (AnalysisLab p.1)

questions.stem: In the CI deck, which methods are named as the MOST effective for discovering defects?

options.text: Manual code reading by the author alone
Design inspections, code inspections, and testing

questions.explanation: The most effective defect-discovery methods are design inspections, code inspections, and testing. (ContinuousIntegration p.8)

questions.stem: What does code-coverage tooling measure in a CI build?

options.text: The number of commits per day
How much of the code the tests exercise (e.g. covered vs uncovered lines/branches)
How many bugs are guaranteed remaining
The compile time of the project

questions.explanation: Coverage tools (Emma, Cobertura, Clover) measure how much code the tests exercise. (ContinuousIntegration p.14)

questions.stem: In CI, why is the source-code repository called the 'source of record'?

options.text: It runs the unit tests itself
The build server only takes code from it, so it is the authoritative version, not any laptop
It stores the defect log only

questions.explanation: The repo is the source of record; the build server only pulls from it, defeating 'works on my box'. (ContinuousIntegration p.6)

questions.stem: The CI definition centres on merging working copies to which target several times a day?
questions.explanation: CI merges all developers' working copies to a shared mainline several times a day. (CILab p.1; ContinuousIntegration p.2)

questions.stem: In CIP, the 'permission to commit' gate is enforced before code reaches which shared resource?

options.text: The repository/mainline that the build server reads from

questions.explanation: CIP gates commits (via architects/code owners) before code lands in the shared repo the build server uses. (TeamProcesses p.16; ContinuousIntegration p.6)
